input.counterSource: https://css-tricks.com/masonry-layout-is-now-grid-lanes/ and https://webkit.org/blog/17660/introducing-css-grid-lanes/ — both confirm the resolved spec uses `display: grid-lanes`, a new display type, superseding the "one new value to grid-template-rows" proposal described in the cited source.
input.evidence:
The claim accurately represents the WebKit "Just Use Grid" proposal as described in the cited webkit.org blog post — that proposal did add only one new value (`collapse`, later framed as `masonry`) to grid-template-rows/columns. However, the claim is refuted on two grounds:

1. OUTDATED/SUPERSEDED: The webkit.org post (blog/16026) was an interim proposal in an ongoing debate. The CSS Working Group reached consensus in January 2025 to adopt `display: grid-lanes` (and `inline-grid-lanes`) as the resolution — a NEW display type value, not merely a new value on grid-template-rows/columns. Safari 26 shipped `display: grid-lanes`; Chrome and Firefox have it behind flags as of mid-2026. The "Just Use Grid / add one new value" option was explicitly NOT the outcome chosen.

2. CLAIM OVERSTATES THE SOURCE: The source says "The Just Use Grid option adds only one new value to CSS" — this describes one competing proposal, not a fact about what CSS masonry IS. The article is making an argument *for* that approach in a debate context. The claim strips the conditional framing and presents it as a factual description of how CSS masonry works, which is an overreach. The final spec uses `display: grid-lanes`, which is a new display type, directly contradicting the claim's assertion that "no new display type" is required.

Contradicting sources: CSS-Tricks "Masonry Layout is Now grid-lanes" (2025), WebKit "Introducing CSS Grid Lanes" (webkit.org/blog/17660), MDN masonry layout docs, and dev.to 2026 guide all confirm `display: grid-lanes` is the adopted syntax — a new display value, not a grid-template-rows/columns extension.
